metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit prerequisite dependency: state that C2 live validation requires lowerclass-creased-fruitbat (hard-delete → re-create wiring) to be merged and deployed first, or restructure the C2 scenario to verify the current behavior (no re-create) with a placeholder that flips to the intended assertion post-fix.
findings.finding: The harness tests C2 (Jira hard-delete → outbound re-create) as a live pass criterion, but lowerclass-creased-fruitbat explicitly documents that the re-create follow-on is BUILT BUT NEVER CONSUMED — the feature is unfinished (`applier.py:469-471` only handles `kind=="suppress_pair"`; the re-create Mutation is constructed but never dispatched). The live C2 test will therefore fail against the current codebase unless lowerclass-creased-fruitbat is completed first. The plan has no stated sequencing dependency on lowerclass-creased-fruitbat being complete, creating a hidden ordering assumption: the validator plan silently assumes the underlying feature it validates already works.
findings.location: Signal definitions section, C2 definition; Acceptance Criteria second bullet 'One observable pass criterion PER matrix scenario (C1-C4...'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define what 'trips' means as a concrete assertion, e.g., `with pytest.raises(BlastRadiusExceeded): reconciler.run(...)` or `assert result.aborted is True and result.abort_reason == 'blast_radius'`.
findings.finding: The blast-radius AC ('a synthetic mass-change trips it') lacks a concrete proving assertion — 'trips' is not defined as a specific observable (exception type, log line, metric, or return code).
findings.location: AC line 2 — blast-radius trip; Signal definitions — blast-radius breaker test

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'lossy labels' explicitly (e.g., 'labels exceeding Jira's 255-char limit are silently truncated on round-trip') and state the expected reconciler behavior (conflict? alert? no-op?) so the test can be written unambiguously.
findings.finding: 'Status round-trip incl. lossy labels' references 'lossy labels' without defining what makes a label lossy or which field exhibits this behavior — this is an implicit acceptance criterion with unstated semantics.
findings.location: AC line 2 — 'status round-trip incl. lossy labels'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a signal definition (e.g., C5) for 'status round-trip incl. lossy labels' in the Signal definitions section — specify which Jira fields constitute 'lossy', the write/read sequence, and the observable pass condition — so the AC criterion is fully grounded in described work.
findings.finding: The AC criterion 'status round-trip incl. lossy labels' has no corresponding signal definition, scenario setup, or body description explaining what test creates/validates a status round-trip and what 'lossy labels' means in this context. This is an orphan criterion the implementer cannot satisfy without guessing.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'One observable pass criterion PER matrix scenario (... status round-trip incl. lossy labels ...)' vs Signal definitions section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a C5 signal definition: 'C5 (status round-trip / lossy labels): after writing a status transition + label set that Jira truncates or transforms (e.g., labels with special chars), the reconciler reads back the canonical form, stores it as the new baseline, and emits no spurious conflict on the next pass. Pass condition: diff between written and read-back values appears in reconciler log as INFO not CONFLICT.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a signal definition for 'tombstone/grace' in the Signal definitions section — specify the trigger (e.g., soft-delete, grace-period flag), the observable reconciler behavior during the grace period vs. after expiry, and the pass condition — so the AC criterion is grounded in described work.
findings.finding: The AC criterion 'tombstone/grace' has no corresponding signal definition, scenario setup, or body description explaining what tombstone or grace-period behavior is being tested, what Jira and local state is arranged, and what observable outcome constitutes a pass. It is an orphan criterion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'One observable pass criterion PER matrix scenario (... tombstone/grace ...)' vs Signal definitions section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the full CREATE/RETIRE lifecycle of `ProvenanceLedger` entries within the test harness: specify who seeds baseline entries before each test pass, how entries are scoped to the test run (e.g., unique key prefix), and how teardown removes or invalidates them.
findings.finding: The plan references `ProvenanceLedger` as the mechanism for baseline-seeding echo suppression, but never defines the CREATE/RETIRE lifecycle of ProvenanceLedger entries within the test harness context — who seeds them before the test pass, how they are isolated between concurrent test invocations, and whether they are cleaned up by the teardown fixture or left to accumulate.
findings.location: Signal definitions — Echo suppression; Harness section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a step in the harness setup that seeds a unique-keyed ProvenanceLedger entry per test run (e.g., keyed by `test_run_id`), and add an explicit teardown step that deletes/expires those entries. Document that the echo suppression check queries only the entries created in the current run.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit signal definitions (what is observed, what constitutes pass) for the four missing matrix scenarios: status round-trip incl. lossy labels, tombstone/grace, idempotency (as a standalone scenario), and blast-radius trip — matching the level of detail in C1–C4.
findings.finding: The AC promises 'One observable pass criterion PER matrix scenario (C1-C4, echo, status round-trip incl. lossy labels, tombstone/grace, idempotency mutation_count==0, blast-radius trip)' — roughly 8–9 scenarios — but the Signal definitions section only provides observable criteria for C1, C2, C3, C4, and echo suppression (5 scenarios). Status round-trip (incl. lossy labels), tombstone/grace, standalone idempotency, and blast-radius trip have no corresponding signal definition, observable assertion, or pass criterion specified in the plan body.
findings.location: Signal definitions section; Acceptance Criteria — 'One observable pass criterion PER matrix scenario'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the 'Signal definitions' section with entries like: 'C5 (status round-trip): after writing status X to Jira, a reconciler pass reads it back; local status == X and no lossy-label mutation in the diff. C6 (tombstone/grace): a tombstoned local ticket is not re-created in Jira within grace_period; after grace_period a hard-delete is issued. C7 (blast-radius): acting_fraction > max_acting_fraction triggers a BRIDGE_ALERT and halts the batch; zero mutations applied.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add named signal definitions (analogous to C1–C4) for 'status round-trip incl. lossy labels', 'tombstone/grace', and standalone 'idempotency' scenarios, each specifying the exact observable (which field/event is checked, what assertion passes/fails).
findings.finding: The 'Signal definitions' section defines observable pass signals for C1–C4 and echo suppression, but three matrix scenarios required by the AC ('status round-trip incl. lossy labels', 'tombstone/grace', 'idempotency mutation_count==0' as a standalone scenario) have no corresponding signal definition specifying WHAT is observed and HOW it is measured. A test author implementing the harness has no specified observable criterion for these cells, leaving them unverifiable.
findings.location: Signal definitions section + AC line: 'One observable pass criterion PER matrix scenario (C1-C4, echo, status round-trip incl. lossy labels, tombstone/grace, idempotency mutation_count==0, blast-radius trip)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 'Signal definitions': S1 (status round-trip): after a local status change is pushed, Jira issue status matches the mapped value AND the reverse sync produces the same local status on the next pull; lossy labels: fields dropped in translation are logged in a reconciler warning, not silently discarded. S2 (tombstone/grace): a tombstoned binding survives for the configured grace period (assert binding still present at T+grace−1s) then is purged (assert gone at T+grace+1s). S3 (idempotency): a second reconciler pass over the just-written state emits mutation_count==0 (no field writes, no comment writes).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the C1 signal definition: the conflict record is a `Mutation` with `action == MutationAction.conflict` (serialized as `{"action": "conflict", "direction": ..., "target": ..., "payload": ..., "provenance": ...}`) — there is no `reason` field; update the assertion to check `mutation.action == 'conflict'`.
findings.finding: The C1 signal definition asserts 'differ emits a conflict record (reason="conflict")' but no field named 'reason' with value 'conflict' exists anywhere in the reconciler codebase. Conflict mutations are represented as Mutation dataclass instances with action=MutationAction.conflict; serialized they have keys direction/action/target/payload/provenance — no 'reason' key.
findings.location: Signal definitions section: 'C1 (conflict signal): differ emits a conflict record (reason="conflict")'
findings.suggested_fix: Change C1 definition to: 'differ emits a Mutation with action=="conflict" when local!=baseline AND jira!=baseline on a mirrored field; verify by asserting any(m.action == MutationAction.conflict for m in mutations).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the C3 signal definition: the current codebase prints field-drop warnings to stderr only (dispatch_one.py:484-489); no BRIDGE_ALERT is emitted — either (a) add a BRIDGE_ALERT write for allowlist-drops as prerequisite work and call it out in the plan, or (b) redefine the C3 observable as a stderr log assertion.
findings.finding: The C3 signal definition asserts 'a deduped BRIDGE_ALERT fires when an outbound field is dropped by the update allowlist,' but the actual code at dispatch_one.py:484-489 only prints to stderr (`print(..., file=sys.stderr)`) when fields are dropped — no BRIDGE_ALERT event file is written. The BRIDGE_ALERT mechanism does not exist for field-allowlist-drop.
findings.location: Signal definitions section: 'C3 (field-allowlist-drop alert): a deduped BRIDGE_ALERT fires when an outbound field is dropped by the update allowlist'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the exact module, class, and method to monkey-patch for C4 (e.g., `acli_subprocess._run_acli` vs. `acli_rest.AcliRestMixin._direct_rest_*`) and specify the exception type/shape the patch must inject to exercise the real backoff path, resolving the subprocess-vs-REST ambiguity documented in lambswool-shaded-eider.
findings.finding: The C4 signal definition specifies 'a synthetic 429 (monkey-patched adapter returns 429 + Retry-After: 2)' without naming the adapter module/class/import path to patch. Critically, acli_subprocess._run_acli raises subprocess.CalledProcessError (not urllib.error.HTTPError), while the existing retry-with-backoff helper in acli_subprocess.py only catches urllib.error.HTTPError (the REST path). These two layers have incompatible exception types; an implementer cannot write a correct monkey-patch without resolving which layer the test targets.
findings.location: Signal definitions section — C4: 'a synthetic 429 (monkey-patched adapter returns 429 + Retry-After: 2 on the first call)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the exact monkey-patch target for C4: specify that the harness must patch `_call_with_backoff`'s wrapped `fn` to raise `urllib.error.HTTPError(429)` with a `Retry-After: 2` header (the only code path where Retry-After is mechanically honored), NOT `_run_acli` where CalledProcessError carries no headers.
findings.finding: The C4 signal definition names an unnamed 'adapter' as the monkey-patch target ('monkey-patched adapter returns 429 + Retry-After: 2 on the first call'), but there are two structurally different 429 paths in the codebase: `_call_with_backoff` (handles `urllib.error.HTTPError` objects that carry `.headers`, so Retry-After honoring is mechanically possible) and `_run_acli` (raises `subprocess.CalledProcessError` with no `.headers`, so Retry-After is unavailable). If the executor patches `_run_acli` to raise CalledProcessError with returncode=429, the Retry-After honoring cannot be tested because that error type has no headers — the harness would prove nothing about the `Retry-After: 2` backoff and could silently pass without ever testing the intended behavior. lambswool-shaded-eider itself calls this an 'unverified capability' for the subprocess path.
findings.location: Signal definitions section, C4 definition: 'a synthetic 429 (monkey-patched adapter returns 429 + Retry-After: 2 on the first call)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the C4 signal definition, replace 'monkey-patched adapter' with: 'patch the `fn` argument passed to `acli_subprocess._call_with_backoff` to raise `urllib.error.HTTPError(code=429)` with `headers={"Retry-After": "2"}` on the first call, then succeed; assert `time.sleep` was called with a delay ≥2s and the final call succeeds.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the C4 monkeypatch target ambiguity: name the specific module and function to patch (distinguishing the REST `_call_with_backoff` path from the subprocess `_run_acli` path), confirm that lambswool-shaded-eider's Retry-After probe has landed before the live harness runs C4, and document what observable behavior (which code path) the C4 assertion actually exercises.
findings.finding: The C4 scenario specifies 'monkey-patched adapter returns 429 + Retry-After: 2' but never identifies WHICH adapter to patch. The codebase has two distinct retry mechanisms: `_call_with_backoff` (handles `urllib.error.HTTPError` with `.headers`, REST path, acli_subprocess.py:236-292) and `_run_acli` (subprocess/CalledProcessError path, acli_subprocess.py:337-444, which has NO Retry-After parsing). Patching the wrong layer produces a test that validates code the reconciler never exercises on a live Jira call via ACLI. The underlying lambswool-shaded-eider ticket itself explicitly flags Retry-After availability on the subprocess path as 'UNVERIFIED CAPABILITY,' meaning the live-harness C4 assertion may be testing a path that doesn't yet exist.
findings.location: Signal definitions / C4 scenario; lambswool-shaded-eider ticket; acli_subprocess.py:236-292, 337-444
findings.suggested_fix: Add a prerequisite gate: C4 live-harness scenario must only run after lambswool-shaded-eider is closed (probe result recorded + implementation merged). Name the patch target explicitly, e.g. `monkeypatch.setattr(acli_subprocess, '_call_with_backoff', ...)` for the REST path or `monkeypatch.setattr(acli_subprocess, '_run_acli', ...)` for the subprocess path, with a clear comment stating which reconciler code path is being exercised.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the C4 monkey-patch target: name the exact class/module to patch (e.g., `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` vs. `acli_subprocess._call_with_backoff`), note that `_call_with_retry` does NOT currently read `Retry-After`, and either (a) add Retry-After support to `_call_with_retry` as a precondition for this test or (b) restate C4 to assert only 'retries after backoff delay' without the Retry-After-specific claim.
findings.finding: The C4 signal definition asserts 'reconciler backs off >=2s' in response to a 'monkey-patched adapter returns 429 + Retry-After: 2'. The codebase has TWO separate retry paths: (1) dispatch_one._call_with_retry (the primary reconciler path) catches JiraAPIError(429) with a fixed delay schedule [1, 2, 4]s — it does NOT read or honor Retry-After at all; (2) acli_subprocess._call_with_backoff catches urllib.error.HTTPError and does honor Retry-After (acli_subprocess.py:266-276). The plan names neither the target adapter class nor module. If the monkey-patch targets _call_with_retry (the path a live reconciler exercise would trigger), the Retry-After header is structurally inaccessible (JiraAPIError carries no headers attribute), so the '>=2s from Retry-After: 2' assertion would either be untestable or accidentally pass due to the fixed-delay schedule — not because Retry-After is honored. This is an asserted external capability that is contradicted for the most likely integration target.
findings.location: Signal definitions / C4 (429/Retry-After)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Signal definitions / C4 block, add: 'Target: `acli_subprocess._call_with_backoff` (REST urllib path) — this path reads `Retry-After`; the primary `_call_with_retry` path in `dispatch_one.py` uses fixed delays and does not. If C4 must cover the full reconciler stack, add `Retry-After` parsing to `_call_with_retry` as a prerequisite task and add its ticket reference here.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add signal definitions (setup, observable, pass criterion) for 'status round-trip incl. lossy labels' and 'tombstone/grace' scenarios, matching the C1–C4 format in the Signal definitions section.
findings.finding: The AC (line 2) mandates a pass criterion for 'status round-trip incl. lossy labels' and 'tombstone/grace' matrix scenarios, but neither appears in the Signal definitions section — no setup protocol, observable, or pass criterion is defined anywhere in the plan for these two scenarios.
findings.location: AC line 2: 'One observable pass criterion PER matrix scenario (C1-C4, echo, status round-trip incl. lossy labels, tombstone/grace, …)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add two entries to the Signal definitions section: (1) 'Status round-trip': set a Jira status via ACLI, run a reconcile pass, assert the local ticket's status field matches and that no label data is lost (define 'lossy' concretely — e.g., labels present before transition are present after). (2) 'Tombstone/grace': soft-delete a local ticket, run a reconcile pass, assert the Jira issue is in the expected grace state (not hard-deleted) and a tombstone marker is written locally.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the exact monkeypatch target for C4 (module path + function: either `rebar_reconciler.acli_subprocess._call_with_backoff` for the REST/urllib path or `rebar_reconciler.acli_subprocess._run_acli` for the subprocess path) and resolve the Retry-After availability question per the lambswool-shaded-eider probe result before writing the assertion.
findings.finding: The C4 test specifies 'monkey-patched adapter returns 429 + Retry-After: 2' but does not identify which adapter to patch. The codebase has two structurally different retry mechanisms: `_call_with_backoff` (urllib.error.HTTPError path, can read `exc.headers.get('Retry-After')`) and `_run_acli` (subprocess.CalledProcessError path, which has no HTTP headers and cannot observe Retry-After). The lambswool-shaded-eider ticket explicitly flags Retry-After availability on the subprocess path as 'UNVERIFIED'. No decision rule is given for which path to patch.
findings.location: Signal definitions section, C4 definition: 'a synthetic 429 (monkey-patched adapter returns 429 + Retry-After: 2 on the first call) -> reconciler backs off >=2s and retries successfully'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a harness protocol for the ProvenanceLedger: specify when it is instantiated (per-scenario or per-pass), how the baseline is seeded before the echo-suppression assertion, and how it is reset between re-runs to satisfy the 're-runnable' AC.
findings.finding: The echo suppression signal's pass criterion (`mutation_count==0 on the next pass over just-written data`) depends on the ProvenanceLedger being pre-seeded in the harness, but the plan gives no protocol for how the harness creates, populates, resets, or retires ProvenanceLedger entries between test scenarios — making the assertion's precondition underspecified.
findings.location: Signal definitions section, 'Echo suppression': 'field/status writes are baseline-seeded via ProvenanceLedger (NOT a literal mark) — verify by mutation_count==0 on the next pass over the just-written data'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the exact adapter class/module (e.g., `jira_client.py::JiraHTTPAdapter`) where the 429 monkeypatch is applied, so the executor does not choose an unintended layer.
findings.finding: The C4 monkeypatch target is unnamed: 'monkey-patched adapter returns 429' does not identify the adapter class/module/import path where the patch is applied, forcing the executor to make an undocumented design choice.
findings.location: Signal definitions — C4 (429/Retry-After)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to C4: 'Monkeypatch `reconciler.jira_adapter.JiraHTTPAdapter.send` (or the equivalent module path) to return `(429, {"Retry-After": "2"})` on the first call only.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a requirement to document the bilateral teardown pattern, leaked-artifacts.log schema, and delete-permission probe contract in an ADR or CLAUDE.md section so future integration test authors can follow the established harness contract.
findings.finding: The novel bilateral cleanup pattern, the leaked-artifacts.log convention, and the teardown-probe contract are not captured in any ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc update. Future test authors extending this harness have no documented contract for how cleanup is guaranteed or how leaks are tracked.
findings.location: Harness (guaranteed bilateral teardown); Acceptance Criteria (isolation documented)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete secrets mechanism (e.g., 'GitHub Actions secret `JIRA_TEST_TOKEN` injected as env var `JIRA_TOKEN`') so the executor can configure it without a design decision.
findings.finding: The secrets manager for credential injection is unspecified ('a secret/secrets manager; never logged, never committed') — 'secrets manager' is a placeholder that defers the choice of mechanism to the executor.
findings.location: Isolation, credentials, blast-radius section
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'via a secret/secrets manager' with the specific mechanism, e.g., 'injected as the environment variable JIRA_TOKEN from a GitHub Actions repository secret named JIRA_TEST_TOKEN.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify the exact location where `mutation_count` is surfaced (e.g., `ReconcileResult.mutation_count` returned by `reconciler.run()`) and write the assertion as `assert result.mutation_count == 0`.
findings.finding: 'mutation_count==0' is referenced as a proving observable for echo suppression but the plan does not name the module, log key, metric, or return value that surfaces this count, making the criterion unverifiable without discovering/inventing the instrumentation.
findings.location: Signal definitions — Echo suppression; AC line 2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete post-teardown assertion to the teardown-under-failure test: e.g., `assert client.get_issue(jira_id)['status'] == 'not_found'` AND `assert local_store.get(local_id) is None` (or equivalent 404 check) after the RuntimeError is injected.
findings.finding: The teardown-under-failure AC has no concrete proving command: 'injects a RuntimeError after create and asserts BOTH artifacts are still cleaned' describes intent but not the assertion (e.g., what API call or DB query confirms the artifact no longer exists post-error).
findings.location: AC line 1 — 'teardown-under-failure test'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a post-run 'zero artifacts' proof command, e.g., `acli list --project TEST --label 'rebar-test-run:<run_id>' | jq -e '.issues | length == 0'`, as a mandatory final step in the harness.
findings.finding: 'ZERO surviving artifacts on success' has no named proving command to verify Jira-side deletion, leaving the end-state claim unmeasurable at run time.
findings.location: AC line 3 — Teardown-failure fallback

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split AC line 2 into one AC per scenario (C1, C2, C3, C4, echo-suppression, status-round-trip, tombstone/grace, idempotency, blast-radius) so each can be independently marked done with its own proving command.
findings.finding: AC line 2 bundles 8+ distinct scenario outcomes (C1-C4, echo, status round-trip incl. lossy labels, tombstone/grace, idempotency, blast-radius trip) into a single acceptance criterion, making it an epic-of-epics that cannot be evaluated as a coherent single observable outcome.
findings.location: AC line 2 — 'One observable pass criterion PER matrix scenario'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact attachment mechanism and command, e.g., `acli attach --issue REB-NNN --file test-report.xml` as a required final harness step, and add an AC that this command exits 0.
findings.finding: 'Attaches a JSON/JUnit report to the ticket' has no described mechanism (ACLI attachment? CI artifact upload? file written to workspace?), making this criterion non-deterministically verifiable in-session.
findings.location: AC line 2 — 'attaches a JSON/JUnit report to the ticket'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Signal definitions whether 'idempotency mutation_count==0' is a superset scenario (any reconciler-authored write, not only echo writes) distinct from echo suppression, or explicitly fold mutation_count==0 into the echo signal definition and remove the standalone 'idempotency' AC entry to eliminate the near-duplicate.
findings.finding: The echo suppression signal definition states that field/status write echo is verified by 'mutation_count==0 on the next pass over the just-written data', and the AC separately lists 'idempotency mutation_count==0' as a distinct matrix scenario. These two entries appear to measure the same observable (mutation_count==0 after a reconciler-authored write), creating a near-duplicate that will produce either redundant tests or confusion about which scenario covers which behavior.
findings.location: Signal definitions — 'Echo suppression' paragraph; Acceptance Criteria — 'idempotency mutation_count==0'
findings.suggested_fix: If idempotency is intended as a broader scenario (applies to any field write, not just echo), add a separate signal definition: 'C6 (idempotency): after any reconciler-authored write, a second reconciler pass over the same data produces mutation_count==0.' Restrict echo suppression's mutation_count==0 to the echo-specific subcase only.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that each `delete_issue` call in the teardown retry loop explicitly asserts `result == {"status":"deleted"}` (or equivalent success check), not merely the absence of an exception, so that acli.py-style soft-fail 403s are caught and trigger the leaked-artifacts.log path.
findings.finding: The teardown retry-with-backoff loop does not specify that each `delete_issue` return value is validated for success (vs. a soft-fail 403 that acli.py:716-720 swallows silently). The setup probe validates the permission once at startup, but the actual per-artifact teardown calls in the retry loop may treat a swallowed PermissionError (which returns something other than an exception) as success and fail to log the artifact as leaked.
findings.location: Harness (guaranteed bilateral teardown) — Teardown-failure fallback; Setup probe section; acli.py:716-720 reference
findings.suggested_fix: In the teardown helper, after each `client.delete_issue(issue_id)` call, add: `if result.get('status') != 'deleted': raise RuntimeError(f'soft-fail delete for {issue_id}: {result}')` — this makes the retry-with-backoff trigger on soft-fail, and exhaustion still routes to leaked-artifacts.log + non-zero exit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify an atomic/serialized append mechanism for `leaked-artifacts.log` (e.g., file locking, a dedicated log-collector process, or OS-level atomic append with O_APPEND) to ensure concurrent teardown workers cannot silently lose leak records.
findings.finding: `leaked-artifacts.log` is a shared file appended to by the teardown fallback, but the plan specifies no atomic-write or locking mechanism. Under parallel test execution (e.g., pytest-xdist), concurrent appends from multiple workers can interleave or corrupt log lines, and the 'non-zero exit on any leak' guarantee may be unreliable if the write races.
findings.location: Harness — Teardown-failure fallback
findings.suggested_fix: Use Python's `fcntl.flock` around the append, or route all leak records through a single pytest session-scoped fixture that collects them in memory and writes the file once at session teardown.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the Harness teardown-failure fallback, confirm whether the new inline retry-with-backoff (3x) should reuse or wrap an existing helper (`_call_with_backoff` / `_call_with_retry`) or explain why a new inline loop is preferred (different semantics: append-to-log + exit-non-zero vs raise).
findings.finding: The Harness section specifies a new inline 'retry-with-backoff (3x)' for teardown cleanup calls without referencing the two existing retry-with-backoff implementations already in the codebase (`_call_with_backoff` in `acli_subprocess.py:236` and `_call_with_retry` in `dispatch_one.py:100`). While teardown semantics differ (log-and-exit vs raise), the plan does not explain why a new inline loop is preferred over adapting or wrapping an existing helper — leaving an implicit NIH risk in the test harness.
findings.location: Harness section — 'Teardown-failure fallback: each cleanup call is retry-with-backoff (3x)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note: 'teardown retry wraps `client.delete_issue` directly (not via `_call_with_retry`) because exhaustion must log+exit rather than raise — a thin `_cleanup_with_retry(fn, *args, log_file, max=3)` helper in the harness module avoids duplicating the delay arithmetic.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the setup-probe rationale: `acli.py:716-720` handles 404 (not_found idempotent return), NOT 403 — the 403 path at lines 721-723 already **raises** PermissionError rather than swallowing it; update the plan to cite the correct lines and accurate behavior.
findings.finding: The plan's setup probe rationale claims 'acli.py:716-720 swallows PermissionError', but reading those lines shows they handle the 404/not_found path (returning {status: not_found}) — a completely different code path. The 403/PermissionError handling is at lines 721-723 and it RAISES PermissionError rather than swallowing it. The probe design is grounded in an inverted characterization of the code.
findings.location: Setup probe section: 'assert client.delete_issue returns {"status":"deleted"} (NOT a 403 soft-fail — acli.py:716-720 swallows PermissionError)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'acli.py:716-720 swallows PermissionError' with 'acli.py:721-723 raises PermissionError on 403, so delete_issue will not silently succeed on a permissions failure — but the setup probe still validates that the test service account actually has delete rights before the harness runs.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the setup-probe rationale: `acli.py:716-724` raises `PermissionError` on 403 (does NOT swallow it); update the probe description to reflect that the probe validates sufficient permissions produce `{'status':'deleted'}`, not that it works around a silent swallow.
findings.finding: The plan's Setup Probe section states 'acli.py:716-720 swallows PermissionError' as justification for the delete-permission probe, but the actual code at acli.py:716-724 RAISES PermissionError on 403 (lines 721-723: `raise PermissionError(msg) from exc`). The file and line range exist, but the behavioral claim is inverted — the code surfaces the error rather than swallowing it. The probe rationale is built on a false premise about the named code location.
findings.location: Harness section — 'Setup probe' paragraph: 'NOT a 403 soft-fail — acli.py:716-720 swallows PermissionError'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'NOT a 403 soft-fail — acli.py:716-720 swallows PermissionError' with 'acli.py:721-723 raises PermissionError on 403 — the probe confirms the service account has Delete permission (so the raise path is never hit in normal teardown)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale for the chosen harness vehicle: explain why pytest autouse fixture is preferred (e.g., 'aligns with existing tests/external/ live test patterns, gives Python-native retry and AcliClient access, integrates naturally with JUnit XML report generation') so implementers do not re-evaluate the choice.
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for the chosen harness architecture (pytest autouse fixture + ACLI client vs. bash trap harness or a direct REST teardown loop). The 'Why' section explains why live validation is needed, but not why this specific mechanism is the right implementation vehicle — a reader cannot tell whether pytest+fixture was chosen over bash+trap for testability, reuse of existing test infrastructure, or Python-native retry logic.
findings.location: Why section and Harness section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Audit which of the C1–C4/echo/idempotency scenarios genuinely require live Jira state vs. can be covered by the existing cassette infrastructure, and justify the live-only choice (or adopt a hybrid: cassettes for logic, live for state-dependent scenarios like C2 hard-delete).
findings.finding: The plan proposes a home-rolled bilateral teardown harness (pytest autouse fixture + finally/trap, retry-with-backoff, leaked-artifacts.log) as a novel pattern for live external integration testing, without acknowledging that a cassette/VCR infrastructure already exists in the codebase (`tests/mocks/jira-cassette-loader.py`). The plan never justifies why the live-wire approach is preferred over extending the cassette approach for the scenarios that can be replayed deterministically (C1, C3, echo suppression, idempotency) and reserving live calls only for scenarios requiring real Jira state (C2 hard-delete, blast-radius).
findings.location: Harness section; tests/mocks/jira-cassette-loader.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the acli.py:716-720 citation and the probe rationale: 403 raises PermissionError (not a silent soft-fail at delete_issue); if caller-level swallowing is the concern, identify the actual caller that suppresses it and probe at that level.
findings.finding: The plan cites `acli.py:716-720` as the location where `PermissionError` is 'swallowed,' using this to justify the delete-permission probe. But reading `acli.py:716-724` in the actual codebase shows the code RAISES `PermissionError` on a 403, not swallows it. The justification for the probe is built on a misread of the prior art.
findings.location: Harness / Setup probe section; acli.py:716-724
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the probe assertion with: call `delete_issue` and assert no `PermissionError` is raised (i.e., the test service account actually has Delete permission), rather than asserting the return value shape which is already guaranteed by the function contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the access posture of `leaked-artifacts.log`: specify its write location, confirm it contains only bare artifact IDs (no API error payloads), exclude it from CI artifact uploads or scope it to admins-only, and document its retention/deletion policy.
findings.finding: The `leaked-artifacts.log` file introduced by the harness teardown-failure fallback has an undeclared access posture: the plan does not specify where this file is written, who can read it, whether it is retained as a CI artifact, or whether Jira API error payloads embedded in teardown failure messages might appear in it. In a CI environment, build artifacts are often downloadable by all repository contributors, creating a potential exposure surface for session/error context.
findings.location: Harness section — 'Teardown-failure fallback: ... append the artifact id to leaked-artifacts.log'
findings.suggested_fix: Write leaked-artifacts.log to a path outside the default artifact upload glob (e.g., `/tmp/rebar-leaked-artifacts.log`), ensure teardown appends only the artifact ID string (not raw exception/API-response text), and if CI artifact upload is desired, add an explicit `if-no-files-found: ignore` upload step scoped to a protected environment.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare and enforce log-hygiene on the harness execution path: specify `set +x` before any ACLI/credential-bearing shell commands in the trap, confirm the pytest fixture does not echo env vars, and assert the C4 monkey-patched adapter's response logging does not include Authorization headers.
findings.finding: The new test harness (pytest autouse fixture / bash trap) introduces a Jira-credential-bearing execution path whose log hygiene posture is undeclared. The plan states the token is 'never logged' but does not specify the mechanism enforcing this on the new code path — e.g., no `set +x` guard before ACLI invocations in bash, no assertion that pytest's output-capture mode does not echo the token value from environment introspection, and no mention of whether the monkey-patched adapter (C4 test) logs the synthetic 429 response headers which could contain auth context.
findings.location: Harness section — 'pytest autouse fixture' / 'bash trap cleanup EXIT'; Signal definitions — 'C4 (429/Retry-After): a synthetic 429 (monkey-patched adapter returns 429 + Retry-After: 2 on the first call)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit `set +x` guard in any bash trap or setup script before credential-bearing commands; in the pytest fixture, assert that `JIRA_API_TOKEN` is consumed only via `os.environ.get()` and is never passed as a CLI argument (use stdin/pipe as the production workflow does); document these constraints in the harness spec.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the acli.py:716-720 citation: the code RAISES PermissionError on 403 (does not swallow it); update the probe rationale to reflect the actual behavior and restate what the probe is actually verifying.
findings.finding: The Setup Probe section cites 'acli.py:716-720 swallows PermissionError' as the reason a delete-permission probe is needed, but the actual code at acli.py:716-724 RAISES PermissionError (it does not swallow it). This stale/incorrect code citation is embedded in an operational decision rule, and an executor relying on it will implement the probe against a behavior that does not exist.
findings.location: Harness section, 'Setup probe': 'assert client.delete_issue returns {"status":"deleted"} (NOT a 403 soft-fail — acli.py:716-720 swallows PermissionError)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'acli.py:716-720 swallows PermissionError' with 'acli.py:716-724 raises PermissionError on 403, which is correct behavior — the probe verifies the service account has Delete permission so teardown calls don't raise PermissionError at all.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
